methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Space & Aerospace (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 25 same-sector peers exist in Space & Aerospace, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.rationale: Public Space & Aerospace P/S median is 42.9x across 6 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
SpaceForest, a Space & Aerospace company led by Rafał Przybyła, completed a public listing via GPW following capital raises that included an IPO and ESA funding. The valuation exercise references a peer universe of 25 same-sector public and private companies, including five public comparables (RKLB, PL, LUNR, ASTS, SATL) and eight private peers anchored by SpaceX, Rocket Lab, Blue Origin, Sierra Space, and Planet Labs.

Two standard triangulation methods proved inapplicable to this analysis. Peer-median pricing was excluded because the narrow filters for same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier comparables yielded fewer than three datapoints—a minimum threshold for statistical reliability. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples could not proceed because SpaceForest's revenue was not available; private companies rarely disclose this metric, and applying the observed Space & Aerospace sector median of 42.9x across five public tickers would be circular without target-specific top-line data.

The absence of a triangulated valuation range reflects a structural gap: while the broader Space & Aerospace sector contains sufficient public trading data and a rich private peer set, SpaceForest occupies a position where stage-specificity and revenue opacity prevent conventional DCF- and multiple-based anchoring. Further analysis requires either disclosure of SpaceForest's revenue run-rate or identification of transaction comparables within the early-stage space infrastructure subsector. Current data supports only a qualitative statement that valuation should reflect positions among peer stages and public multiples, not a quantified range.
